era_context: The Late Roman Republic in acute crisis. The Social War (91–88 BCE) has recently concluded, leaving Italy devastated and Rome's Italian allies newly enfranchised but resentful. Mithridates VI of Pontus is expanding into the Eastern Mediterranean, threatening Roman interests in Asia Minor and Greece. The First Mithridatic War (88–84 BCE) is underway. Domestically, the conflict between the optimates (conservative aristocracy) and populares (reformist faction) is intensifying, with Sulla and Marius as rival military strongmen. This is the eve of the first Roman civil war.

events.title: Treaty of Dardanus: End of the First Mithridatic War
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla, commanding Roman forces in the East, negotiates peace with Mithridates VI of Pontus at Dardanus (modern Dardanelles region). Mithridates agrees to evacuate Asia Minor, Bithynia, and Cappadocia, restoring Roman hegemony in the East. Sulla, however, is forced to accept a relatively lenient settlement because he must return to Italy to confront his political enemies, particularly the faction supporting Marius. This treaty marks the end of Mithridates' first major expansion but leaves him intact as a future threat. The settlement demonstrates the vulnerability of Rome's Eastern position when internal politics demand military resources at home.
key_figures: Lucius Cornelius Sulla (Roman general and politician)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (fragmentary for this period, known through epitomes)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-attested in multiple ancient sources, though written centuries later. The date 84 BCE is secure from chronological synchronization with Roman consular records.
cross_references: Follows from -88-001 (outbreak of First Mithridatic War)
Leads to -83-001 (Sulla's return to Italy and civil war)

events.title: Sulla's Army Marches on Rome; Beginning of First Roman Civil War
events.description: After concluding the Mithridatic War, Lucius Cornelius Sulla leads his legions back to Italy with the intention of seizing power from his political enemies, particularly the faction supporting Gaius Marius and the tribune Sulpicius Rufus. This marks the first time a Roman general has marched an army on Rome itself in open defiance of the Senate and People. The act is unprecedented and shatters the Republican norm that generals must disband their armies before entering the city. Sulla's march triggers the First Roman Civil War (84–82 BCE), which will devastate Italy and fundamentally weaken Republican institutions. The crossing of the Rubicon (metaphorically, though not yet the literal river) represents the point of no return for Republican governance.
events.certainty_note: The event is well-documented in multiple ancient sources. The date 84 BCE is secure from consular chronology, though some sources place the initial march slightly earlier (85 BCE). The conventional date is 84 BCE for Sulla's decisive advance.
cross_references: Part of broader conflict: -83-001 (Battle of the Colline Gate)

events.title: Death of Gaius Marius
events.description: Gaius Marius, the legendary Roman general who reformed the army and defeated Jugurtha and the Cimbri and Teutones, dies in Rome at an advanced age (reportedly 70). Marius had been driven into exile by Sulla's faction and had fled to Africa; he returned to Italy in 87 BCE with the support of the consul Cinna and briefly held power before his death. His death removes a major rival to Sulla but also eliminates a potential moderating force. Marius's legacy—the professionalization of the Roman army through recruitment of landless citizens—has fundamentally altered Roman military and political dynamics, making the rise of military strongmen like Sulla possible. His death marks the end of an era of Republican military heroism and the beginning of the age of civil wars.
events.certainty_note: Marius's death is well-attested. The date is traditionally given as 86 BCE, but some sources place it in 84 BCE. The uncertainty reflects ancient chronological variation; 86 BCE is more commonly accepted by modern scholarship, but 84 BCE appears in some traditions.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Marius's death is disputed: most modern scholars favor 86 BCE rather than 84 BCE, though ancient sources vary. The significance of Sulla's march is sometimes overstated as a 'crossing of the Rubicon' moment—in reality, the Republican system had already been severely strained by the Social War and the conflict between optimates and populares. Sulla's actions were shocking but not entirely unprecedented in the context of late-Republican military strongmen. Some modern historians argue that the 'First Civil War' framing imposes a teleological narrative of decline; others see it as a rational response to institutional breakdown.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 84 BCE is rich but entirely dependent on sources written 100+ years later (Appian, Plutarch, Livy epitomes). No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are generally reliable on major events but may conflate or misdate details. Roman sources are heavily influenced by pro-Sullan or pro-Marian bias depending on the author's era. The perspective is overwhelmingly Roman and elite; the impact on Italian allies and non-elite populations is largely invisible. Eastern sources (Greek, Pontic) are sparse for this period.

graph_edges.note: Treaty with Mithridates freed Sulla to pursue civil war in Italy
Sulla's march on Rome directly precipitated the civil war and the Battle of the Colline Gate
Marius's death occurred during Sulla's advance, removing a key rival
